title: First Unreal Project Setup
instructions: Create your first Unreal Engine project using the class naming convention and the tutorial setup choices.
requiredSteps: Open Unreal Engine using the approved class workflow.
Create a new Games > Blank project.
Leave the project set to Blueprint.
Enable Starter Content.
Name the project using the class naming convention.
Create the project and confirm it opens successfully.
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of project browser or open Unreal project
Screenshot or written confirmation showing correct project name

rubric.description: Complete, polished, on time, and clearly meets or extends the stated requirements.
Complete and meets the stated requirements.
Partially complete or missing required evidence.
Attempted but incomplete, unclear, or not functional.
Not submitted.

extensionChallenge: Create a short note showing where your project folder is saved and how you would reopen the .uproject file.
reflectionPrompt: What project setup choice do you most need to remember next time?

title: Unreal Interface Map
instructions: Identify and label the major Unreal editor panels so you can explain what each one is used for.
requiredSteps: Open the Unreal project from Lesson 01.
Capture a clear screenshot of the editor interface.
Label the Viewport, Toolbar, Outliner, Details panel, and Content Drawer or Content Browser.
Add one short purpose note for each required interface area.
Submit the annotated screenshot or teacher-provided form response.
evidenceRequired: Annotated screenshot or form response identifying viewport, toolbar, outliner, details panel, and content drawer

rubric.description: Complete, polished, on time, and clearly meets or extends the stated requirements.
Complete and meets the stated requirements.
Partially complete or missing required evidence.
Attempted but incomplete, unclear, or not functional.
Not submitted.

extensionChallenge: Add two extra interface areas or shortcuts, such as World Settings, F10, Ctrl+Space, or Default Editor Layout.
reflectionPrompt: Which editor panel do you think will help you stay most organized, and why?

title: Viewport Navigation Scavenger Hunt
instructions: Complete a viewport navigation scavenger hunt using camera movement, focus, view modes, and game view.
requiredSteps: Open the Unreal project and use right mouse plus WASD to navigate.
Use Q and E to move vertically at least once.
Use F to focus on a selected object.
Capture screenshots from at least three different views or camera positions.
Capture one screenshot showing Game View or a different view mode such as Unlit or Wireframe.
evidenceRequired: Screenshots from at least three different views or camera positions
One screenshot showing game view or a different view mode

rubric.description: Complete, polished, on time, and clearly meets or extends the stated requirements.
Complete and meets the stated requirements.
Partially complete or missing required evidence.
Attempted but incomplete, unclear, or not functional.
Not submitted.

extensionChallenge: Record a 20-second screen capture showing smooth movement from one object to another and ending in Game View.
reflectionPrompt: Which navigation shortcut helped you the most today?

title: Starter Scene Transform Challenge
instructions: Build a simple starter scene using starter content objects and transform tools.
requiredSteps: Use starter content objects or simple shapes to build a small scene.
Move at least three objects with the W shortcut or move gizmo.
Rotate at least two objects with the E shortcut or rotate gizmo.
Scale at least one object with the R shortcut or scale gizmo.
Duplicate at least one object using Ctrl+D or Alt-drag.
Capture a screenshot of the completed simple scene and write a short reflection naming at least three tools or shortcuts used.
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of completed simple scene
Short reflection naming at least three tools or shortcuts used

rubric.description: Complete, polished, on time, and clearly meets or extends the stated requirements.
Complete and meets the stated requirements.
Partially complete or missing required evidence.
Attempted but incomplete, unclear, or not functional.
Not submitted.

extensionChallenge: Use snapping intentionally to align objects and add one note explaining where snapping helped your scene.
reflectionPrompt: Name three tools or shortcuts you used, and explain which one helped your scene most.

title: First Scene Cleanup and Evidence Upgrade
skillFocus: Quiz feedback helps identify what needs review
Viewport navigation helps check the scene from useful angles
Transform tools fix placement, scale, and rotation issues
The Outliner helps select, organize, and rename visible objects
Screenshots document evidence for teacher review
instructions: Complete or review Quiz 01, then improve the simple scene you built in Lesson 04 with stronger organization, cleaner object placement, and clearer final evidence.
requiredSteps: Complete or review Quiz 01 for Lessons 01-04.
Open the simple scene built in Lesson 04.
Clean up object placement.
Fix floating or intersecting objects.
Improve the camera angle for the final screenshot.
Organize visible evidence so the scene is easier to understand.
Take a stronger final screenshot.
Write a short reflection naming at least three tools or shortcuts used.
evidenceRequired: Before screenshot, if available.
After screenshot.
Short reflection naming at least three tools or shortcuts.
Optional quiz completion screenshot or teacher checkoff.

rubric.description: Complete, polished, on time, and clearly meets or extends the stated requirements.
Complete and meets the stated requirements.
Partially complete or missing required evidence.
Attempted but incomplete, unclear, or not functional.
Not submitted.

extensionChallenge: Create one portfolio-ready screenshot with a clear camera angle, readable object placement, and no distracting editor clutter.
reflectionPrompt: Name at least three Unreal tools or shortcuts you used, then explain how they improved the scene.

title: Post-Process Exposure Test
skillFocus: Post Process Volume applies camera and image effects
Metering Mode can be set to Manual for more control
Infinite Extent Unbound lets the volume affect the whole level
Bloom, Vignette, and Saturation change the image style
instructions: Duplicate a simple level or create a small test area, build a hut or dark-space exposure example, then use post-process settings to control exposure and one visual effect.
requiredSteps: Create or duplicate a level for post-process testing.
Build a simple hut or dark/bright test space.
Observe auto exposure behavior.
Add or select a post-process volume.
Set metering mode to manual.
Adjust exposure compensation.
Enable infinite extent unbound when appropriate.
Experiment with at least one effect such as bloom, vignette, or saturation.
evidenceRequired: Screenshot showing auto exposure/problem situation.
Screenshot showing manual exposure/post-process result.
Short reflection explaining what changed.

rubric.description: Complete, polished, on time, and clearly meets or extends the stated requirements.
Complete and meets the stated requirements.
Partially complete or missing required evidence.
Attempted but incomplete, unclear, or not functional.
Not submitted.

extensionChallenge: Create two mood versions of the same view, such as neutral exposure and dramatic high-contrast exposure, and label the setting that changed.
reflectionPrompt: Explain which post-process setting changed the scene most and how your screenshots prove it.

title: First Custom Material
skillFocus: The Material Editor uses a node graph
Base Color controls the main visible color
Roughness controls how shiny or matte the surface feels
Metallic controls whether the surface behaves like metal
Apply and Save update the material for the level
instructions: Create a custom material, connect color and scalar values in the material graph, apply the material to an object, and submit evidence of both the graph and the result in the level.
requiredSteps: Create or open a material example level.
Create a new material using naming convention M_.
Open the material graph.
Add a color/vector value to base color.
Add scalar values for roughness and metallic.
Press Apply and Save after changes.
Apply the material to an object.
Optional: add a noise/texture sample to break up roughness or color variation.
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of the material graph.
Screenshot of the material applied to an object.
Short reflection explaining base color, roughness, and metallic.

rubric.description: Complete, polished, on time, and clearly meets or extends the stated requirements.
Complete and meets the stated requirements.
Partially complete or missing required evidence.
Attempted but incomplete, unclear, or not functional.
Not submitted.

extensionChallenge: Create a comparison board with three objects using the same base color but different roughness values.
reflectionPrompt: Explain what Base Color, Roughness, and Metallic each changed in your material.

title: Texture Map Material Build
skillFocus: Texture assets appear in the Content Drawer
Texture samples feed image data into the material graph
Base Color maps carry color information
Roughness and Normal maps carry non-color data
Texture Coordinate and Multiply nodes can adjust tiling or scale
instructions: Import or use provided texture maps, check basic texture settings, create a material using base color, roughness, and normal maps, then adjust tiling or scale using texture coordinates or a multiply node.
requiredSteps: Import or locate a base color texture.
Import or locate a roughness texture.
Import or locate a normal map.
Confirm sRGB is on for color textures.
Confirm sRGB is off for roughness/masks and normal maps.
Confirm normal map compression/settings if applicable.
Create a material using M_ naming convention.
Connect base color to Base Color.
Connect roughness to Roughness.
Connect normal map to Normal.
Use texture coordinate/multiply setup or equivalent to adjust tiling/scale.
Apply and save the material.
Apply it to a floor or object.
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of imported texture assets.
Screenshot of material graph connections.
Screenshot of material applied in the level.
Short reflection explaining what the normal map adds.

rubric.description: Complete, polished, on time, and clearly meets or extends the stated requirements.
Complete and meets the stated requirements.
Partially complete or missing required evidence.
Attempted but incomplete, unclear, or not functional.
Not submitted.

extensionChallenge: Create two versions of the same material with different tiling values and label which one feels more believable for the object scale.
reflectionPrompt: Explain what the normal map adds to the material and why the non-color map settings matter.

title: Material Instance Variation Set
skillFocus: Material instances inherit from parent materials
Parameters expose safe values for editing
Scalar and vector parameters control different kinds of material data
Material instances update faster than rebuilding separate materials
Apply and Save make new parent-material parameters available to instances
instructions: Students complete Quiz 2, convert useful material values into parameters, create material instances, and produce at least two controlled material variations in the level.
requiredSteps: Complete Quiz 2 or record teacher checkoff.
Open the parent material from the previous texture-map workflow.
Convert a scalar value, such as size or roughness strength, into a parameter.
Convert a color value into a vector parameter.
Press Apply and Save so the instance can access the new parameters.
Create a material instance using the MI_ naming convention.
Apply the material instance to an object or floor surface.
Enable and adjust at least two exposed parameters.
Create a second variation with a different readable look.
Capture evidence showing the parent material and the two variations.
evidenceRequired: Quiz 2 completion record or teacher checkoff.
Screenshot of the parent material showing at least two exposed parameters.
Screenshot of at least two material instance variations applied in the level.
Short reflection explaining how a material instance is different from a separate new material.

rubric.description: Complete, polished, on time, and clearly meets or extends the stated requirements.
Complete and meets the stated requirements.
Partially complete or missing required evidence.
Attempted but incomplete, unclear, or not functional.
Not submitted.

extensionChallenge: Create three named variations that could belong to one environment set, such as clean, worn, and stylized.
reflectionPrompt: Explain how a material instance changes workflow speed compared with creating a brand-new material every time.

title: Starter Master Material Variations
skillFocus: Master materials organize reusable material logic
Neutral default values keep instances predictable
Texture parameters allow instances to swap maps
Static switch parameters can enable optional features
Clear naming keeps materials and material instances readable
instructions: Students build or inspect a starter master material, identify reusable parameters, and create controlled material variations from that shared parent workflow.
requiredSteps: Open or create the starter master material.
Check that default values are neutral and predictable.
Expose texture inputs for Base Color, Roughness, and Normal where appropriate.
Add or inspect a Metallic texture option.
Use or identify a Static Switch Parameter for optional metallic behavior.
Press Apply and Save.
Create at least two material instances from the master material.
Change parameters or texture slots to create controlled variations.
Apply the variations to objects in the level.
Capture screenshots that show both the setup and the visible results.
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of the master material or teacher-provided master material parameters.
Screenshot of a material instance with texture or value parameters enabled.
Screenshot of at least two applied material variations in the level.
Short reflection explaining one benefit of a master material.

rubric.description: Complete, polished, on time, and clearly meets or extends the stated requirements.
Complete and meets the stated requirements.
Partially complete or missing required evidence.
Attempted but incomplete, unclear, or not functional.
Not submitted.

extensionChallenge: Design a naming system for a small material library and label which instances would be used for floor, wall, prop, or metal objects.
reflectionPrompt: Explain one way a master material saves time or reduces mistakes in a larger Unreal project.

title: Static Mesh Import and Material Setup
skillFocus: Static meshes are Unreal 3D object assets
Import options affect how an FBX enters the project
Folder organization keeps asset sets readable
Static meshes need materials to look finished
Default material slots make repeated placements easier
instructions: Students import a static mesh, organize its folder, apply or assign a material instance, place the mesh in the scene, and verify that it behaves like a usable Unreal asset.
requiredSteps: Create a folder for the imported asset set.
Import the FBX static mesh into the correct folder.
Review import options and reset to default if needed.
Choose not to auto-create materials if the class workflow uses a master material.
Place the imported mesh in the scene.
Import or locate the needed texture maps.
Create a material instance from the master material.
Assign the correct texture maps to the material instance.
Apply the material instance to the mesh.
Open the Static Mesh Editor and set the default material slot if appropriate.
Capture screenshots of the imported asset and scene result.
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of the imported static mesh in the Content Drawer.
Screenshot of the mesh placed in the scene.
Screenshot of the material instance or default material slot used on the mesh.
Short reflection explaining what to check when an imported mesh looks wrong.

rubric.description: Complete, polished, on time, and clearly meets or extends the stated requirements.
Complete and meets the stated requirements.
Partially complete or missing required evidence.
Attempted but incomplete, unclear, or not functional.
Not submitted.

extensionChallenge: Create a small prop arrangement using at least three duplicates of the imported mesh with different transforms.
reflectionPrompt: Explain what you should check if an imported mesh is the wrong size, has the wrong material, or appears unfinished.

title: Asset Migration Verification
skillFocus: Migration preserves dependencies better than manual file copying
The destination must be the Unreal project Content folder
The Asset Report shows what Unreal plans to copy
Project folders and .uproject files help locate the correct destination
instructions: Students migrate a small asset set from a source Unreal project into the class project, verify that the assets arrived in the destination Content folder, and prove one migrated asset works.
requiredSteps: Open the source project that contains the asset set.
Locate the asset or folder to migrate.
Right-click and choose Migrate.
Review the Asset Report and confirm the listed dependencies.
Find the destination project Content folder.
Select the destination Content folder, not the project root.
Confirm the migration.
Open the destination project.
Locate the migrated folder or assets in the Content Drawer.
Place or open at least one migrated asset to verify it works.
Capture screenshots of the source, migration step, destination, and verification.
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of the source project asset or folder selected for migration.
Screenshot of the migration asset list or confirmation step.
Screenshot of the migrated folder or assets in the destination project.
Screenshot of at least one migrated object placed or opened successfully.
Short reflection explaining why dependencies matter.

rubric.description: Complete, polished, on time, and clearly meets or extends the stated requirements.
Complete and meets the stated requirements.
Partially complete or missing required evidence.
Attempted but incomplete, unclear, or not functional.
Not submitted.

extensionChallenge: Create a small before-and-after note showing what would likely break if only the visible mesh file were copied without dependencies.
reflectionPrompt: Explain why Unreal migrates dependencies with an asset and why the destination Content folder matters.

title: Lumen Lighting Comparison
instructions: Complete or review Quiz 3, then create or use a simple test scene to compare lighting behavior with clear evidence screenshots.
requiredSteps: Complete or review Quiz 3 using the teacher-approved workflow.
Open a simple lighting test scene, migrated lighting example, or teacher-provided scene.
Capture a before screenshot where lighting looks flat, too dark, or not yet improved.
Identify where Lumen or global illumination settings affect the scene.
Adjust the lighting setup, movable light, source radius, exposure, or Lumen-related setting as directed.
Capture an improved screenshot showing clearer bounce light, softer shadow, or more believable lighting.
Upload evidence screenshots to Google Drive and paste share links into DCC Creative Studio.
Write a short reflection explaining how lighting changed the scene.
evidenceRequired: Quiz 3 completion record or teacher checkoff.
Google Drive link to a screenshot showing a lighting setup before adjustment.
Google Drive link to a screenshot showing improved Lumen or global illumination result.
Short reflection explaining how lighting changed the scene.

rubric.description: Complete, polished, on time, and clearly meets or extends the stated requirements.
Complete and meets the stated requirements.
Partially complete or missing required evidence.
Attempted but incomplete, unclear, or not functional.
Not submitted.

extensionChallenge: Create a three-image lighting comparison: no Lumen or weak bounce light, improved Lumen/global illumination, and a dramatic mood version.
reflectionPrompt: Explain how Lumen or global illumination changed the mood, realism, or readability of your scene.

title: Three-Light Mood Study
instructions: Build or open a small scene and create three different lighting setups using at least three light types.
requiredSteps: Open a small scene with at least one object, wall, prop, or focal area.
Create a first lighting setup using a point light.
Create a second lighting setup using a spotlight or rectangle light.
Create a third lighting setup using a directional light, skylight, or environment lighting.
Adjust intensity, color temperature, color, angle, or shadow softness intentionally.
Capture one screenshot for each lighting setup.
Upload screenshots to Google Drive and paste share links into DCC Creative Studio.
Write a reflection explaining which setup created the strongest mood and why.
evidenceRequired: Google Drive link to a screenshot of setup using point light.
Google Drive link to a screenshot of setup using spotlight or rectangle light.
Google Drive link to a screenshot of setup using directional light, skylight, or environment lighting.
Short reflection explaining which setup created the strongest mood and why.

rubric.description: Complete, polished, on time, and clearly meets or extends the stated requirements.
Complete and meets the stated requirements.
Partially complete or missing required evidence.
Attempted but incomplete, unclear, or not functional.
Not submitted.

extensionChallenge: Create a fourth lighting setup inspired by a real place such as a stage, hallway, sunset, studio desk, or game checkpoint.
reflectionPrompt: Which light type made the biggest difference in mood, and which setting caused that change?

title: Sculpted Terrain Study
instructions: Create a landscape, sculpt at least three elevation zones, smooth or refine terrain, and frame a useful screenshot of the result.
requiredSteps: Create or open a clean Unreal map for the terrain study.
Enter Landscape mode and create a basic landscape.
Sculpt at least three elevation zones such as low area, hill, ridge, valley, or platform.
Use Smooth to reduce harsh artifacts or unrealistic bumps.
Use Flatten to create one intentional playable or focal area.
Check the scene from a wide camera angle and a closer terrain angle.
Upload evidence screenshots to Google Drive and paste links into DCC Creative Studio.
Write a reflection naming the landscape tools you used.
evidenceRequired: Google Drive link to a screenshot of Landscape mode or landscape setup.
Google Drive link to a wide shot of sculpted terrain.
Google Drive link to a close-up showing terrain shaping or refinement.
Short reflection naming the landscape tools used.

rubric.description: Complete, polished, on time, and clearly meets or extends the stated requirements.
Complete and meets the stated requirements.
Partially complete or missing required evidence.
Attempted but incomplete, unclear, or not functional.
Not submitted.

extensionChallenge: Add a clear path, overlook, valley, or buildable platform that could support a future mini-world.
reflectionPrompt: Name at least three landscape tools or settings you used and explain what each one changed.

title: Mini-World Environment Pass
instructions: Add foliage or environmental props to a landscape or scene, create a clear player path or focal area, and include a simple Blueprint preview or interaction concept if appropriate.
requiredSteps: Open the landscape or small scene from the previous lesson.
Add foliage or environmental props using Foliage mode, placed static meshes, or teacher-approved assets.
Adjust density, scale, or placement so the scene is detailed but readable.
Create or clarify a player path, focal area, or mini-world destination.
Include a simple Blueprint preview screenshot or written interaction concept if your setup supports it.
Play test or camera test the mini-world from a student/player viewpoint.
Upload evidence screenshots to Google Drive and paste links into DCC Creative Studio.
Write a reflection explaining how the scene became more believable or playable.
evidenceRequired: Google Drive link to a wide screenshot of mini-world environment.
Google Drive link to a close-up screenshot showing foliage or prop detail.
Google Drive link to a screenshot or written description of simple interaction or Blueprint preview.
Short reflection explaining how the scene became more believable or playable.

rubric.description: Complete, polished, on time, and clearly meets or extends the stated requirements.
Complete and meets the stated requirements.
Partially complete or missing required evidence.
Attempted but incomplete, unclear, or not functional.
Not submitted.

extensionChallenge: Add one optional interaction concept, such as a trigger area, checkpoint, collectable, or simple win condition, and describe how it would work.
reflectionPrompt: Explain how foliage, props, path design, or Blueprint preview changed your scene from a terrain study into a mini-world.
